title: Easy Build End Table or Nightstand with Drawer
summary: A 2x4-and-1x8 case with a single euro-slide drawer and a planked 2x8 top — built flush-front to match a modern farmhouse bed, or overhung to match a classic one.
description:
This nightstand is a simple 2x4 leg-and-side box built specifically to carry one drawer on euro-style slides, with a 2x8 planked top doing double duty as both the surface and the visual weight of the piece. The side assemblies go together first — 1x8 sides glued and nailed to short 2x4 uprights, held flush at the top with any unevenness pushed to the bottom, which keeps the visible top edge clean without fussing over the invisible one.

The drawer opening is the one place this build asks for real attention: install both drawer slide cabinet members at the exact same height and front-set position on both sides, or the drawer will run crooked no matter how well everything else is built. Before cutting drawer parts, verify your own opening matches the assumptions this cut list is built on — a 21-1/2" inside width, 3/4" material, and 1/2"-per-side slide clearance — and adjust the drawer box size if any of those don't match your actual build.

The top can be kept flush to the front on all sides for a cleaner modern look, or overhung on the front and sides to match a more traditional farmhouse piece — both are legitimate finishes off the same base.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft or longer
materials.note: Legs — cut to match the width of the 1x8 boards.

cutList.note: Cut to match the actual width of your 1x8 boards.
Cut about 1/4" smaller than your actual front opening.
Do not cut until you measure your actual drawer opening.
Approximate — measure and adjust to fit.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Cut everything except the drawer box now; those parts wait until you have measured the real opening. From the 2x4: four legs 18-1/2" and four leg cleats 7-1/4" — cut the cleats to the actual width of your 1x8, which is nominally 7-1/4" but varies board to board, because a cleat that does not match the side will show as a step at the corner. Those eight pieces come to 103" of 2x4, so a single 8 ft board is about 7" short; the material line allows a longer board, and one 10 ft 2x4 carries all eight with room over. From the 1x8: two sides 18-1/4", one back 23", and the drawer face 22-3/4". From the 2x8: three top boards 30", which side by side give a top 30" wide and about 21-3/4" deep. Square every cut and label the pieces as they come off the saw — the 18-1/4" sides and the 18-1/2" legs are only a quarter inch apart and are easy to confuse.

steps.title: Build the side assemblies
steps.body: Each side assembly is one 18-1/4" 1x8 side with a 7-1/4" 2x4 leg cleat at each end. Run glue on the cleat, set the 1x8 on it with the outside edges lined up, and drive 1-1/4" brad nails through the 1x8 into the cleat. Hold the parts flush at the TOP edge and push any width discrepancy to the bottom — the top edge is what shows under the tabletop and the bottom is hidden, so that is where a mismatch should land. Build two of these and stack them face to face to confirm they are identical before going on.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Apply glue where the legs overlap the sides and attach with 2" brad nails and glue, keeping outside and top edges flush.

steps.title: Mount the drawer slides
steps.body: Install the drawer slide cabinet members at exactly the same height and front-set position on both sides — the front (wheel side) is flush to the front edge of the leg cleat. If they aren't installed identically, the drawer will run crooked.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Set the 23" back board across the rear of the two side assemblies, flush with their top edges, and fasten it with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue into the leg cleats and the legs. That 23" is the case's outside width, so with a 3/4" 1x8 side inside it at each end you are left with the 21-1/2" inside opening the drawer slides are sized for. Measure that opening now, before any drawer part is cut — everything downstream depends on it being what the plan assumes.

steps.title: Build and fit the drawer
steps.body: Confirm your measured opening matches the plan's assumptions — 21-1/2" inside width, 3/4" material, and 1/2" of slide clearance per side, which puts the drawer box at 20-1/2" wide — and adjust the parts if it does not. Cut the 1x4 front and back 19" and the two sides 18"; with the 19" pieces between the two 3/4" sides the box finishes 20-1/2" wide by 18" front to back. Note that the cut list gives the plywood bottom as 20-1/2" x 19": the 20-1/2" is right, but the 19" is an inch long for an 18"-deep box, so cut the bottom to the box you actually built. Assemble the box square, nail and glue the bottom on with 3/4" brad nails, and keep it perfectly flush all round so nothing rubs the slides. Mount the drawer members at the same height on both sides, slide the box in, and check it runs freely before you go any further.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: With the drawer opening and closing smoothly, nail the top boards down. Keep everything flush to the front for a modern look, or overhang the front and sides to match a more traditional piece.

steps.title: Fit the drawer face
steps.body: The face is 22-3/4", a quarter inch under the 23" front opening, which leaves an even 1/8" gap at each side. With the drawer closed, hold the face in position and shim it until the reveal is even at the top and both sides, then glue and pin it with 1-1/4" brad nails driven through the face into the drawer box front. Keep the nails well clear of wherever the knob or pull will go, or you will hit one when you drill. Sand everything, easing the top edges and corners where hands land, and finish.

images.alt: Simple end table or nightstand with one drawer on euro slides
